coredata.dc:title: Microwave-assisted green synthesis of carbon dots derived from wild lemon (Citrus pennivesiculata) leaves as a fluorescent probe for tetracycline sensing in water
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Venugopalan, P.
Vidya, N.
coredata.dc:description: Herein, we report an eco-friendly biomass based completely greener microwave assisted synthesis of carbon dots from wild lemon leaves having superior photo-luminescent properties with moderately good quantum yield. The carbon dots synthesized (LLCDs) were characterized by high resolution transmission electron microscopy, fluorescent, UV–vis absorption, Fourier transform infrared and Raman spectroscopic techniques. The quenching of native fluorescence of LLCDs observed with tetracycline antibiotic was made use to make it as a fluorescent probe. Tetracycline is widely used as a drug to treat various bacterial infections and as a growth promoter in variety of farming fields like aquaculture and feedstock’s. The excessive usage of this antibiotic in farming fields caused severe water pollution and micro level detection of tetracycline in water system is highly demanded for the protection of health and environment. Based on the selective interaction of tetracycline with LLCDs, a simple and cost effective analytical method was proposed to detect tetracycline in water with satisfactory parameters. The limit of detection is 0.42 µM with a linear range of 0 to 27. 27 µM. The mechanism of quenching was investigated through various experiments and finally accredited to static quenching. Furthermore, the same method has been practically applied for tetracycline detection in natural water resources with acceptable recoveries ranging from 95.56 % to 101.93 %.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescent probe
Tetracycline
Static quenching mechanism
Antibiotic water pollution

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of tetracycline in environmental water samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Sree Neelakanta Government Sanskrit College Pattambi

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge Sree Neelakanta Government Sanskrit College Pattambi, University of Calicut and FIST program of DST for providing the facilities. SAIF Cochin and PSG IAS Coimbatore are acknowledged for experimental support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a): Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of LLCDs; (b): The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern; (c): TEM image at lower scale; (d): Dynamic light scattering size distribution curve of LLCDs.
(a): Raman spectrum of LLCDs; (b): FTIR spectrum of LLCDs.

(a): UV–vis absorbance spectrum of LLCDs; (b): Fluorescence spectrum (inset shows photographs of LLCDs solution under normal light (left) and UV light (right)); (c): Excitation dependent spectra of LLCDs; (d): Excitation and emission spectra of LLCDs.

(a): Variation fluorescence intensity of LLCDs with storage time; (b): Variation of luminescence with ionic strength (with different concentration of KCl);(c): Relative fluorescence response of LLCDs with different tetracycline antibiotics (oxytetracycline, chlorotetracycline and tetracycline); (d): Relative fluorescence intensity of LLCDs in absence and presence of tetracycline with different foreign substances (from 1 to 30: LLCDs, urea, glucose, ascorbic acid, alanine, phenyl alanine, aspartic acid, leucine, valine, Cl−1, SO4 2-, NO3 –, CN–, Br-, I-, CO3 2–, K+, Zn2+, Na+, Mn2+, Cd2+, Ca2+, Pb2+, Co2+, Hg2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, florfenicol, streptomycin and sulfamerazine).

(a): Steady decrease of fluorescence intensity with increasing concentration of LLCDs (inset displays photographs of LLCDs before (left) and after (right) the addition of tetracycline; (b) Plot of relative fluorescence F0/F intensity with tetracycline solution; (c) Fluorescent decay spectra of LLCDs and LLCDs with tetracycline (LLCDs@TC); (d): UV–vis spectra of LLCDs, tetracycline, LLCDs with tetracycline (LLCDs@TC) and summed spectra of LLCDs and tetracycline (LLCDs + TC).
Detection of tetracycline in different real water samples.

head.ce:title: Microwave-assisted green synthesis of carbon dots derived from wild lemon (Citrus pennivesiculata) leaves as a fluorescent probe for tetracycline sensing in water

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Sree Neelakanta Government Sanskrit College (Affiliated to University of Calicut), Pattambi, Kerala 679306, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Sree Neelakanta Government Sanskrit College (Affiliated to University of Calicut)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Sree Neelakanta Government Sanskrit College Affiliated to University of Calicut, Pattambi, Kerala 679306, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, we report an eco-friendly biomass based completely greener microwave assisted synthesis of carbon dots from wild lemon leaves having superior photo-luminescent properties with moderately good quantum yield. The carbon dots synthesized (LLCDs) were characterized by high resolution transmission electron microscopy, fluorescent, UV–vis absorption, Fourier transform infrared and Raman spectroscopic techniques. The quenching of native fluorescence of LLCDs observed with tetracycline antibiotic was made use to make it as a fluorescent probe. Tetracycline is widely used as a drug to treat various bacterial infections and as a growth promoter in variety of farming fields like aquaculture and feedstock’s. The excessive usage of this antibiotic in farming fields caused severe water pollution and micro level detection of tetracycline in water system is highly demanded for the protection of health and environment. Based on the selective interaction of tetracycline with LLCDs, a simple and cost effective analytical method was proposed to detect tetracycline in water with satisfactory parameters. The limit of detection is 0.42 µM with a linear range of 0 to 27. 27 µM. The mechanism of quenching was investigated through various experiments and finally accredited to static quenching. Furthermore, the same method has been practically applied for tetracycline detection in natural water resources with acceptable recoveries ranging from 95.56 % to 101.93 %.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: From the day since antibiotics has been realized to treat infectious diseases, it has been used tremendously and effectively in medicinal system for both human as well as animals. Moreover, their advantages have also been utilized as a growth promoter in variety of fields like agriculture, aquaculture, and livestock’s [1]. In past few years the usage of drugs has been elevated drastically, this elevation indirectly makes impacts on the environmental system. Tetracycline has been considered as the most widely used one among the antibiotic family; it belongs to broad- spectrum antibiotics and acts effectively against both Gram-positive as well as Gram-negative bacteria. In addition, it can efficiently active towards several bacterial infections. Nevertheless, tetracycline absorption is comparatively difficult for animal metabolism. So most them are excreted to the nature as in the form of parental compound [2,3]. The unrestrained uses of tetracycline and its releases to the nature constitute major threat to environmental system. The tetracycline excretion created by human as well as animals primarily pollutes the water bodies, since most of such wastes finally reach to the water system. Use as growth promoter in the farming fields extremely enlarges the issue and the water sources near these farms are highly affected. In spite of the fact that tetracycline is not easily causes chronic toxicity in typical circumstances, durable exposure to the tetracycline contaminated water has been leads to acute toxicity in non-targeted beings [2]. These antibiotic residues are heavily toxic to microbes and it can accumulate in the different organs of the body and leads to several diseases like, allergies, gastrointestinal diseases, nephrotoxicity and severe reactions in central nervous system and blood [4]. Hence, trace level detection of tetracycline in water system is reckoned with significant importance for the protection of health and environment.

Up to this point of time, the major methods used to detect tetracycline are Delvotest SP [5], high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC)[6], the blue-yellow screening test [7], capillary electrophoresis [8], liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry [9], electrochemical immunosensors [10], and enzyme immunoassay [11]. The above mentioned methods demands highly sophisticated and expensive instruments as well as time consuming. Moreover, these techniques have relatively lesser selectivity and sensitivity. So it is highly required to implement a new advantageous method, which can resolve all these problems. Recently, fluorescent based assays for detection of numerous aquatic pollutants, including antibiotic residues with high selectivity and sensitivity are received considerable importance and several reports are coming from this aspect [12–16].

Among the several fluorescent probes, carbon nanomaterial especially, carbon dots based fluorescent systems having highest acceptability because of its attractive properties like high level biocompatibility, water solubility and excellent and tunable optical properties and these fluorescent probes are more eco-friendlier than the other fluorescent probes under use.Carbon dots have been considered as the eco-friendliest and efficient candidate from carbon nanomaterial sector, and their particle size falls under 10 nm and exhibits strong and bright fluorescence [17–19]. The unique properties of carbon dots as mentioned earlier along with higher chemical stability, lesser cytotoxicity, eminent and strong photoluminescence, they have been applied in many fields including biomedical and nanomedicine [20–23], electrochemical sensing and electrocatalysis [24,25], photocatalysis [26], and biosensing, bioimaging and fluorescence sensing [27–29].

Recently, natural products based carbon dots received commendable importance than the other organic precursor based systems. It is basically owing to its nontoxicity, renewability, bio-availability, low-cost and greener nature [30,31].In the present work, a partially bio-waste material, wild lemon (Citrus pennivesiculata) leaves was selected as the precursor. It is well established that lemon fruit can be successfully used for carbon dots synthesis [32]. The lemon and leaves of lemon shares major of its constituents and possessing variety of medicinal values, hence the materialization of this partially wasting highly available precursor through completely greener and facile method, imparts value addition to the precursor. The synthesis follows strict green protocol completely devoid of any hazardous chemicals or solvents. It is found to be advantageous over other methods because of its silent features like short reaction time, direct and homogeneous microwave heating, and environmental benign nature [33,34].

In this piece of work, highly fluorescent carbon dots were successfully synthesized from wild lemon leaves, an agro-waste by green and facile microwave method. Selective quenching in the fluorescence of prepared system on introducing tetracycline was studied well and the mechanism is attributed to static quenching. The efficiency of the system as a fluorescent probe for the detection of tetracycline in water was carried out. The good level of precision and accuracy with detection limits in micromolar range are promising to use the system as a fluoroprobe for detecting tetracycline, a primary pollutant found in natural water resources.

Leaves of wild lemon were collected from the garden in the month of August. The leaves were cleaned thoroughly with distilled water to remove the dust particles. All reagents used were of analytical grade. Metal salts (potassium chloride, potassium cyanide, sodium carbonate, potassium bromide, potassium iodide, zinc acetate, sodium chloride, manganese chloride, cadmium nitrate, lead nitrate, cobalt nitrate, calcium chloride, mercuric chloride, copper sulfate and ferric chloride hexahydrate) and antibiotics were procured from Merck Ltd., India. Amino acids,urea and glucose were purchased from Nice Pvt. Ltd., India. All standard solutions were prepared using double distilled water.

The surface morphology of carbon dots was evaluated by transmission electron microscope (TEM) images and the corresponding selected area electron diffraction pattern (SAED) were used to study the crystallographic nature of the carbon dots. Both were captured using JOEL JEM 2100 operating at 200 KV using LaB6 electron gun. The average particle size and size distribution were obtained from the Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) analysis and it was carried out using DLS Zetasizer instrument (Malvern Panalytical). The extent of graphitization and defects of the prepared system was analyzed using Raman spectrum and it was recorded by a confocal Raman Microscope (WiTec alpha 300, Germany) with 532 nm laser excitation line. Fourier transform infrared spectrum (FTIR) was used to study the surface functionalities of the carbon dots, and was recorded with PerkinElmer Fourier transform infrared spectrum (FTIR) spectrometer in the KBr pellet method. The spectrum was recorded over the range of 4000 – 500 cm−1 with average scan rate of 64 scans. All the absorbance studies of the prepared system were performed using PerkinElmer UV–vis spectrophotometer, with a scan range of 200 – 800 nm. The fluorescence measurements were carried out by the use of PerkinElmer FL-6500 fluorescence spectrophotometer. All the fluorescence studies were conducted at 25 °C. Luminescent nature of the system was primarily confirmed using ROTEK U.V. Cabinet and all the fluorescent photographs of the prepared system were taken under the same cabinet.

Carbon dots were prepared by a facile, one step microwave pyrolysis method. Briefly, about 30 g of cleaned wild lemon leaves were crushed with domestic grinder and then by mortar and pestle, it was transferred to a beaker with 30 mL hot distilled water and subjected to stirring by the aid of magnetic stirrer for 30 min of time. After filtration and centrifugation of 10 min, the supernatant solution was exposed to microwave irradiation for 20 min at 500 W with periodic cooling in each 5 min. The resulting dried residue was magnetically stirred for 5 min with 30 mL of distilled water and filtered. In order to remove large particles, the as obtained solution was centrifuged at 1500 rpm for 20 min. The clear pale yellow coloured supernatant liquid was then subjected to dialysis against distilled water for 48 h by a dialysis membrane with molecular cut off 3KDa. The solution emitting bright greenish blue fluorescence under UV light denotes the formation of carbon dots.
Fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of the prepared carbon dots as well as carbon dots solution with tetracycline was measured as per the following equation (1):

QYS = QYST[MS/MST](Ƞ2 S/Ƞ2 ST) (1) where QYS and QYST are the quantum yield of the carbon dots solution and standard, respectively. Quinine sulfate (In 0.1 M H2SO4, with QY = 0.54) was used as the standard for this measurements. MS and MST are the slopes of linear plot between integrated intensity of fluorescence with UV–vis absorbance of the carbon dots and standard, respectively. Ƞ is the refractive index of the solvents used to dissolve the carbon dots and standard.

Fluorescence behaviour of the carbon dots were studied by recording the fluorescence spectra at excitation of 330 nm, with emission maxima centred at 520 nm. The excitation dependence of the system was examined by recording the spectra at different excitation wavelength ranging from 300 to 410 nm, giving different emission maxima with varying intensities, revealing excitation dependent behaviour of LLCDs. The procedure for the quenching studies on LLCDs with tetracycline is as follows: 2 mL of the LLCDs solution was introduced in to the quartz cuvette, fluorescence spectrum was recorded at an excitation of 330 nm. Afterwards a definite amount of tetracycline solution was added by the aid of micropipette, after an incubation of 2 min the corresponding fluorescence spectrum was recorded. The same procedure was repeated with different concentration of tetracycline solution in ascending manner and fluorescence spectra were recorded in every time at the same excitation wavelength. The selectivity studies on the system were carried out by introducing 100 µL of 1 mM solutions of different foreign substances including biomolecules (urea, glucose, ascorbic acid, alanine, phenyl alanine, aspartic acid, leucine, valine), anions (Cl−1, SO4 2-, NO3 –, CN–,Br-, I-, CO3 2–), metal ions (K+, Zn2+, Na+, Mn2+, Cd2+, Ca2+, Pb2+, Co2+, Hg2+, Cu2+, Fe3+) and antibiotics (florfenicol, streptomycin andsulfamerazine) in absence and presence of tetracycline to 2 mL of LLCDs. The selectivity investigations also follow the same procedure of quenching studies. The corresponding effect on fluorescence behaviour was monitored by recording the spectra at an excitation wavelength of 330 nm.

Water samples were collected from the river and pond, filtered to remove impurities and followed by centrifugation at 1500 rpm for 20 min of time. To detect the tetracycline in real samples, pre-treated water samples were spiked with different concentration of standard tetracycline solutions and were introduced to LLCDs solution. Briefly 10 µL of standard tetracycline solution (498, 998, 1454 µM) was primarily added to 1 mL of pre-treated water sample and then mixed with 1 mL of the probe solution and after incubation of 2 min, the fluorescence spectrum of the probe with spiked sample was recorded, again 10 µL of tetracycline solution was added to the same solution and fluorescence was monitored and the process was continued till the total volume of added tetracycline was about 100 µL. The same procedure was followed for all the three different concentrations of tetracycline in each environmental sample. The fluorescence spectra of the probe with tetracycline spiked water samples were recorded using the same instrument and conditions as above.

LLCDs were synthesized by the microwave heating of the green wild lemon leaves. The water extract of the leaves contains plenty of acids and several carbohydrates. The exact formation mechanism of carbon dots from organic compounds is still obscure in nature. The microwave irradiation initiates the bottom-up synthesis of carbon nano materials from the small molecules present in the extract. It follows several steps like, condensation, polymerization and carbonization followed by fluorescent carbon dot formation via nuclear burst. The acidic groups present in the precursor plays an important role in the formation process [33,35].

The chemical as well as optical properties of the LLCDs were explored using the following methods. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were directly utilized to detect the morphology and particle size of the LLCDs. As seen in Fig. 1 they possess almost spherical character and the average size and particle size distribution of the prepared LLCDs was determined through the dynamic light scattering (DLS) results and it was about 4.08 nm and 1.62 nm to 8.16 nm, respectively. The corresponding curve was displayed in Fig. 1(d).The crystallographic nature of the LLCDs was primarily confirmed from the SAED pattern. Since the SAED pattern having neither clear design nor bright spots, implies that the prepared LLCDs having very poor crystalline nature or it is nearly amorphous [36].

Raman spectrum of the LLCDs displayed in Fig. 2 (a), exhibits two strong peaks. The band at 1339 cm−1 representing the D band whereas band at 1572 cm−1 was attributed to G band. The ID/IG ratio of the system was calculated from this spectrum and is found to be greater than one (1.32), where ID and IG are the integrated intensity of the D and G band, respectively. This ratio demonstrates the extent of disorder associated with the material. It indirectly representing the sp3/sp2 ratio and it reveals the high level of structural defects present in the LLCDs. Microwave irradiation during the synthesis of LLCDs may be the reason for this extended defects in the system. Moreover, this ratio also supports the poor crystalline as well as lesser graphitization nature of the prepared LLCDs [37].

FTIR spectrum was used to reveal the surface functionalities associated with the prepared LLCDs. As seen in Fig. 2(b), a strong broad band centred at 3274 cm−1 is ascribed to OH stretching vibrations and small bands at 2918 cm−1, 2825 cm−1 representing CH stretching where as one at 925 cm−1 attributed to CH bending vibrations [38]. A shoulder peak at 1632 cm−1 could be ascribed to CC stretching vibration, and band at 1051 cm-1assigned to CO stretching vibrations of carboxylic as well as alcoholic functionalities. In addition, bands at 1556 cm-1and 1401 cm-1 was arises due to symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibrations associated with COO– [39]. The above results implies that LLCDs surface contains mainly –OH, –COOH, CO and CC functionalities [40]. Thus the FTIR spectrum disclose that LLCDs surface consists of hydroxyl and carboxyl groups and being hydrophilic these groups also contributes toward the water solubility and stability of the system in water.

The UV–vis spectrum of the LLCDs (Fig. 3 (a)) has three shoulders at 207, 268, and 315 nm. The first two peaks were ascribed to π-π* transitions of CC bonds whereas the third one assigned to n-π* transition of CO bonds present at the surface of LLCDs [41]. Multi absorption transition modes may be the reason for the weak and asymmetric shoulder peaks [42]. The fluorescence spectrum of the LLCDs is shown in Fig. 3(b), exhibiting emission maxima centred at 520 nm when excited at 330 nm. The present system shows excitation dependent fluorescence behaviour and it was evaluated by monitoring fluorescence spectra of the LLCDs recorded at different excitation wavelength (300 – 410 nm). The results of the same were depicted in Fig. 3(c). The variation of intensity and emission maxima of the LLCDs excited at different wavelength may ascribed to polydispersity and surface functionalities of LLCDs [43]. From the excitation dependence study it is clear that the system possessing highest intensity when excited at 330 nm and its emission spectra was centred at 520 nm and these values are opted as excitation and emission wavelength of the LLCDs, respectively. The corresponding excitation and emission peaks were shown in Fig. 3(d).The prepared LLCDs showing bright greenish blue fluorescence in UV lamp of 365 nm, and is given in the inset of Fig. 3(b).The quantum yield of LLCDs was found to be about 7.2 % as calculated by the equation (1) with quinine sulphate in 0.1 M H2SO4 with 54 % quantum yield as standard.

The effect of storage time and conditions on fluorescence stability of prepared system was studied. Fig. 4 (a) shows the normalized fluorescence intensity of the system vs time of storage (sample was stored at 4 °C).The system has only infinitesimal changes even after 10 weeks of time, revealing that, it exhibits strong resistance against photo bleaching, since. The intensity of the LLCDs were gradually declined as it was stored at above 25 °C, however it is stable up to one week. The instability at this temperature may attributed to increased tendency for aggregation of particles, leads to particle size increment [44]. Moreover, the system was subjected to continue UV irradiation to study fluorescence stability under UV light of 365 nm lamp inside the UV cabinet, and it was stable up to 2 h after that the fluorescence intensity was slowly reduced. The fluorescence stability of as prepared LLCDs was also investigated in variable ionic strength. The fluorescence intensity of the LLCDs almost same in different concentration of KCl and is shown in Fig. 4(b), indicates that the system was stable at higher ionic strength, and thus ensure that it can be applicable in distinct ionic conditions. The obtained results suggested that, the system exhibits admirable fluorescent stability.

In virtue of superior fluorescent behaviour of the prepared system, it was tested whether it can be applied as a fluorescent probe or not. To study the selectivity of the LLCDs several biomolecules, anions, metal ions and some of the antibiotics which are widely used in aquaculture and veterinary farms including tetracycline were used for this purpose. Relative fluorescence response of LLCDs in presence and absence of tetracycline with these substances was given in Fig. 4(d). It clearly reveals that the fluorescence of the LLCDs get reduced predominantly by the addition of tetracycline. However Fe3+ and Cu2+ exerts small quenching behavior and is may be attributed to the interaction of these metal ions with the surface functionalities of carbon dots and this quenching percentage is very small as compared with that of tetracycline [45]. By the addition of Fe3+ and Cu2+ about 19 % and 12 % reduction in the native fluorescence of the probe was observed, similarly florfenicol and streptomycin causes about 19 % reduction in fluorescence and this may be due to the hydrogen bond formation with the surface functionalities [46]. But the same time it is about 40 % in the case of tetracycline. All others exert minute and negligible quenching effect. Even though, none of these substances exerts significant interference in tetracycline detection and is clear from the interference studies. Besides, other antibiotics belongs to tetracycline class were also tested and all were shows similar quenching effects (Fig. 4(c)). Therefore, tetracycline was opted as a representative of the tetracycline antibiotic family. The sensitivity of the system towards tetracycline was studied by recording the fluorescence spectra of LLCDs with different concentration of tetracycline solution (0 – 42.86 µM). The spectra showing quenching of fluorescence by addition of tetracycline was displayed in Fig. 5 (a), the inset of the same figure representing the LLCDs solution before and after the addition of tetracycline, the fluorescence quenching is very clear from the photographs. Moreover, the fluorescence quantum yield of the LLCDs solution with tetracycline was also determined by the same method as explained earlier for quantum yield calculation and is found to be 2.5 % by using quinine sulphate as reference standard solution. The decrease of fluorescence quantum yield of LLCDs from 7.2 % to 2.5 % by the addition of 42.86 µM of tetracycline solution, indicate the formation of non-fluorescent species within the system. To study the relationship between fluorescence intensity signals with concentration of quencher, a plot of F0/ F with concentration of tetracycline in µM was plotted and was shown in Fig. 5(b),where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of LLCDs and LLCDs with tetracycline, respectively. It reveals that the F0/ F ratio was proportionally increased with increasing concentrations of tetracycline. This curve is found to be linear from 0 to 27.27 µM of tetracycline with R2 = 0.9979.The limit of detection (LOD) of the probe was calculated from the linear part of the plot by considering S/N ratio as 3 by using the equation 3σ / κ, where σ is the standard deviation of the measurements and κ is the slope obtained from the linear equation (F0/F = 0.07242 CTC + 0.9664) and it was about 0.42 µM. These parameters were found to be comparable with recent reports. The fluorescent nanoprobe designed in this work exhibits unique merits in operational simplicity, fastness, sensitivity and environmental friendly nature. The present system carries several dominance behaviours than the similar reports, such as eco-friendly microwave assisted synthesis without using any hazardous chemicals, value addition to an agro waste material via materialization and its successful implementation as fast responsive, label free and economical fluorescent probe for the selective detection of primary pollutant, tetracycline antibiotic in water. The method described for the probe fabrication from partial bio-waste material involves extremely simple separation process thereby saving energy and time.The micromolar level of detection limit and good linear range of detection were comparable with recent reports, which are majorly prepared from chemical precursors and using several toxic and hazardous solvents and follows time consuming synthetic and separation process. On the basis of above study results, the present system can be employed as magnificent candidate for detection of tetracycline in environmental water samples.

As stated earlier the natural fluorescence of LLCDs was reduced on addition of tetracycline to the working solution after an incubation of 2 min. A number of experiments were carried out to investigate the underlying mechanism of this quenching. In general fluorescence quenching follows five major mechanisms, named as inner filter effect (IFE), dynamic quenching, static quenching, Forster energy resonance transfer (FRET) and photo-induced electron transfer (PET)[47]. Primarily, the chance of inner filter effect was investigated. The present system and quencher does not follow the essential condition for IFE; there no effective overlapping between neither excitation,nor emission spectra of LLCDs with absorption spectrum of tetracycline in water. To further explore the nature of quenching, the fluorescence decay studies were conducted by recording decay spectra of LLCDs without and with tetracycline. The results were shown in Fig. 5(c).The average lifetimes of above two were 7.80 and 7.62 ns, respectively. Reveals that the mechanism was not belongs to FRET, PET or dynamic quenching, since all of them resulting significant change in average lifetime [41,47]. Furthermore, investigation on quenching mechanism was conducted by recording UV–vis spectra of LLCDs, tetracycline, LLCDs with tetracycline (LLCDs@TC). The results shown in Fig. 5(d), the summed spectrum does not match with the LLCDs@TC spectrum. There are absorption changes to the system on addition of tetracycline. Specifically, it was noted that the tetracycline intensity at around 365 nm was altered and it may be the result of the interaction between LLCDs with tetracycline. Besides, there exists a small peakat around 388 nm in the LLCDs@TC spectrum, which is not present in LLCDs or tetracycline spectrum. These results could indicate the formation of a ground state complex between LLCDs and tetracycline. Altogether the sensing nature of the LLCDs with tetracycline meets almost all criteria of static quenching mechanism. As per the static mechanism, a non-fluorescent complex will be formed when a round state fluorophore (here LLCDs) interact with a quencher. Finally, all the results from above discussions guided to the fact that the mechanism behind the quenching of native fluorescence of LLCDs by tetracycline mainly having static quenching nature [42].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of tetracycline in environmental water samples
ce:section.ce:para: With regard to the tetracycline activated fluorescence quenching, LLCDs were successfully applied to detect tetracycline in real water samples. River water from NilaRiver and nearby Pond water was selected as the environmental samples for the analysis. The real water analysis follows standard addition method. Fluorescence spectra of the water samples were recorded after the pre-treatment without tetracycline, then the samples were spiked with different concentration of tetracycline in µM. Afterwards the fluorescence spectra of the samples with LLCDs nanoprobe were recorded, and fluorescence intensity in each measurement was carefully monitored. Results from the analysis are given in Table 1 .and the recovery % of the analysis was ranging from 95.56 to 101.96, and relative error ranged between 0.97 and 4.43 %.The obtained recovery percentage is highly acceptable and more accurate and the error percentages are less on comparing with similar assays [42]. Hence, the good level of statistical parameters implies that the suggested method may provide accurate and reliable results in determination of tetracycline in water samples.

In summation, a biogenic, highly fluorescent carbon dots were synthesized by a truly greener methodology through a single step microwave heating process. The nature based precursor wild lemon leaves solely used as the source for the preparation. The as obtained LLCDs manifested several excellent features and were explored through various characterization methods. Investigation results display the selective and sensitive interaction of LLCDs with tetracycline antibiotic. The sensing of tetracycline via fluorescence quenching shows comparatively lower limit of detection (0.42 µM) and maintain a good linear response with concentration of tetracycline. The quenching was further examined by different methods and finally ascribed to static fluorescence quenching mechanism. Based on this, a simple and facile analytical method was illustrated for the detection of tetracycline in water systems. Furthermore, assay time of the proposed method for the detection of tetracycline was comparatively very low and it falls under 5 min of time including 2 min of incubation time for a single analysis. The synthesis as well as sensing procedures strictly follows green chemistry protocols. The designing of label-free fluorescent probe for the detection of tetracycline in aquatic environment has been done without using any hazardous chemicals or expensive instruments. The real water analysis shows comparatively good statistical parameters. In short, a highly fluorescent, biocompatible, label-free nanoprobe was developed without any chemical reagents in an easiest way and was successfully employed for tetracycline detection in environmental water samples with good recoveries.
P. Venugopalan: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. N. Vidya: Investigation, Writing – original draft.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge Sree Neelakanta Government Sanskrit College Pattambi, University of Calicut and FIST program of DST for providing the facilities. SAIF Cochin and PSG IAS Coimbatore are acknowledged for experimental support.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Antibiotic Pollution in the Environment: A Review, CLEAN – Soil, Air
Water.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Gothwal, T. Shashidhar, Antibiotic Pollution in the Environment: A Review, CLEAN – Soil, Air, Water. 43 (2015) 479–489. https://doi.org/10.1002/clen.201300989.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A review on pollution situation and treatment methods of tetracycline in groundwater
Sep. Sci. Technol.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Dai, M. Liu, J. Li, S. Yang, Y. Sun, Q. Sun, W. Wang, L. Lu, K. Zhang, J. Xu, W. Zheng, Z. Hu, Y. Yang, Y. Gao, Z. Liu, A review on pollution situation and treatment methods of tetracycline in groundwater, Sep. Sci. Technol. 55 (2020) 1005–1021. https://doi.org/10.1080/01496395.2019.1577445.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fate and transport of antibiotic residues and antibiotic resistance genes following land application of manure waste
J. Environ. Qual.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.C. Chee-Sanford, R.I. Mackie, S. Koike, I.G. Krapac, Y.-F. Lin, A.C. Yannarell, S. Maxwell, R.I. Aminov, Fate and transport of antibiotic residues and antibiotic resistance genes following land application of manure waste, J. Environ. Qual. 38 (2009) 1086–1108. https://doi.org/10.2134/jeq2008.0128.
